title: I don't make videos. My AI pipeline does.

style.name: inline
style.description: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palet…

chunks.summary: you build things (beat 1 of 3 — dense open)
chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Stick figure at laptop, just clicked publish on something. Expectant face. Orange shirt. Clean, minimal.

beats.narration: You build things.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Same stick figure now proudly holding up the finished artifact \u2014 a small tablet/phone/poster showing the completed work. Big smile, one thumb up. Small sparkle lines around the object to signal 'look what I made.'.

beats.narration: It came out great.

chunks.prompt: Same stick figure, standing still with the artifact off to the side. Arms slightly up in a shrug. Eyes looking around blankly, confused 'now what?' expression. Deadpan, empty background.

beats.narration: Now what?

chunks.summary: getting attention is a separate job (beat 2)
chunks.prompt: Split frame: left side stick figure coding (labeled BUILDING), right side stick figure at a podium with a megaphone (labeled MARKETING). Arrow or barrier between them.

beats.narration: Getting attention for what you built is a separate job.

chunks.prompt: Three micro-panels horizontally: SKILLS (paintbrush + camera icons), TIME (clock), ENERGY (drained battery). Quick rhythm visual for the tricolon.

beats.narration: Different skills. Different time. Different energy.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 shifts to a new idea about the reach of what gets built, which is not a direct continuation of the idea about different skills, time, and energy.

chunks.prompt: Single-beat deadpan punchline chunk. Stick figure staring at a laptop showing '0 views' or empty analytics. Deflated expression, arms down.

beats.narration: Even if you have all three — skills, time, energy — the harder problem is: most of what gets built doesn't reach anyone.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a specific solution (AI) that doesn't directly follow the implicit question of how to ensure reach, requiring a bridge to connect the problem to the proposed solution.

chunks.prompt: Builder hopefully typing into an AI prompt box labeled 'make me a video.'

beats.narration: The obvious fix is to have an AI make the video for you.

chunks.prompt: MEME SPIKE — 'this is fine' dog sitting in a burning room (K.C. Green, reference use). Full-frame, replaces anchor style for deadpan punctuation. See ASSET_RULES.md Punchline Chunk Carve-Out.

beats.narration: That produces slop.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 provides a specific consequence of the problem stated in beat N, but the transition would be smoother with a bridge that explicitly connects the slop to the style drift.

chunks.prompt: Three tiled illustration panels in wildly different art styles (cartoon, watercolor, photorealistic). Same subject, totally different styles. Off-anchor deliberately.

beats.narration: Style drifts from scene to scene.

narration.notes: The viewer is likely thinking about the implications of style drift, and the introduction of pacing randomness seems like a separate issue without a clear connection.

chunks.prompt: Clock face with erratic, jittery hands. Or a timeline with unevenly spaced beat markers — some scrunched, some stretched. Chaotic rhythm visible.

beats.narration: Pacing is random.

narration.notes: The second beat introduces a practical problem that isn't directly implied by the first beat, requiring a bridge to connect the randomness to the difficulty of iteration.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: A refresh/regenerate icon above a stack of wildly different-looking video thumbnails. Each regeneration = a whole new video, not an edit.

beats.narration: You can't iterate without the whole thing regenerating into a different video.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces the solution (spoolcast) to the problem (inability to iterate without regenerating the whole video) mentioned in beat N, but the transition is abrupt and requires a bridge to connect the problem to the solution.

chunks.prompt: Clean title card. 'spoolcast' hand-drawn lettering, small stick figure builder next to it holding a tool.

beats.narration: This is spoolcast.
It's an architecture for making real short-form illustrated video.
Built for people who build things and don't want marketing to be a second job.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces the intended audience, which is a natural follow-up to the viewer's implicit question about who would use such an architecture. · Beat N+1 introduces a specific example and timeline, which is not a direct continuation of the general statement in Beat N.

chunks.prompt: Builder at laptop, relaxed, glancing at a chat notification with mp4 thumbnail.

beats.narration: In the next seven minutes, we'll walk through the pipeline that made that clip for under three dollars.
And the agent layer that runs it in the background while you keep building.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (the agent layer) without a clear connection to the cost and process of the pipeline mentioned in beat N. · Beat N+1 introduces a new concept about the fix not being a smarter AI, which is orthogonal to the idea of the agent layer running in the background.

chunks.summary: not smarter AI — division of labor
chunks.prompt: AI-brain character overwhelmed, juggling 6 labeled jobs (story, script, images, animation, camera, render). Balls spilling out.

beats.narration: The fix isn't a smarter AI.
It's a division of labor.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (the generic approach) without clearly connecting it back to the division of labor established in beat N.

chunks.prompt: Same overwhelmed AI-brain with a red X marking one of the labeled jobs. No way to fix just that one item.

beats.narration: The generic approach asks one model to do everything.
Story. Script. Images. Animation. Camera. Timing. Rendering.
When anything's wrong, there's no atomic unit you can change and re-run.
That's why the output feels slopped together.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 lists out specific tasks, which the viewer is likely curious about after hearing that the generic approach asks one model to do everything. A bridge is needed to make this transition smooth and clear. · Beat N+1 introduces a new idea about the complexity and interdependence of the video production process, which is not a direct continuation of simply listing the components.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Single-beat deadpan punchline. AI-brain looking straight at camera. Flat expression.

beats.narration: Structurally, it was.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (atomic driver) without a clear connection to the structural aspect mentioned in beat N, requiring a bridge to maintain coherence.

chunks.summary: one atomic driver — the shot list
chunks.prompt: Screenshot/PNG of the actual spoolcast-explainer shot-list.xlsx, opened in Numbers. Pan across rows. Real artifact, no AI illustration.

beats.narration: In spoolcast, there's exactly one atomic driver.
The script.
Specifically, a shot list — a spreadsheet with one row per sentence the narrator will say, grouped into scenes.
Change one row. Only the artifacts downstream of that row get rebuilt.
Everything else stays untouched.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces the script as the atomic driver without a clear transition, which could be confusing for the viewer. · Beat N+1 answers the implicit question about how the shot list functions in practice, but a bridge is needed to make this transition smooth and clear for the viewer. · Beat N+1 introduces a new concept about the roles and rules of layers, which is not a direct continuation of the idea that everything else stays untouched.

chunks.prompt: Four small layer icons (IMAGE/ANIMATION/VOICE/RENDER) each with a 'NO IMPROVISING' sticker. Flat.

beats.narration: Every other layer has exactly one job. And one rule.
It cannot improvise.

narration.notes: Beat N introduces a limitation, and beat N+1 provides a solution or explanation for how to mitigate that limitation, requiring a bridge to connect the problem to the solution.

chunks.prompt: Title card / section divider. 'ANTI-SLOP PROCESSES' hand-lettered. Clean.

beats.narration: Here's what specifically keeps the script from being slop.

chunks.prompt: Screenshot of real pilot shot-list.json with beats indented under chunks. Syntax-highlighted. Shows 4 beats wrapped in one chunk visually.

beats.narration: One. Images are grouped by chunk, not by beat.
A chunk is one to six sentences that share a visual moment.
One illustration covers the whole chunk.
Saves the budget. Keeps the eye from whipping around.

chunks.prompt: The real pilot C1-nano-1k.png displayed full-frame. Chaotic ad-wall. On screen as proof the technique worked.

beats.narration: Two. The image doesn't illustrate a sentence.
It shows the chunk's narrative throughline.
A chunk about ad saturation gets one image of an ad-wall.
Not a picture of every individual word.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (narrative throughline) that needs to be explicitly connected to the idea that the image doesn't illustrate a sentence. · Beat N+1 introduces a specific example (an ad-wall) that isn't a direct continuation of the general concept of a narrative throughline, requiring a bridge to connect the two. · Beat N+1 introduces a clarification about the image that isn't directly connected to the initial thought about ad saturation, requiring a bridge to maintain flow.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: A timer at 15 seconds with a yawn icon appearing. Then an arrow to a second image replacing the first.

beats.narration: Three. Chunks have a hard cap around fifteen seconds.
Past that, the static image goes dead.
Split it. New image.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a solution or next step, but without a bridge, the viewer may not immediately connect it to the problem just stated.

chunks.prompt: A single sentence in quotes, highlighted, lifted out of a page. The rest of the page fades.

beats.narration: Four. Every sentence has to survive on its own.
If a line only makes sense when you read the one before it, that's an essay.
Rewrite until each beat can be lifted out and still land.

chunks.prompt: Side-by-side: 'ROAS' with audio waveform labeled 'R-O-A-S' (letter by letter, sad), vs 'roe-ass' with waveform labeled 'roe-ass' (clean).

beats.narration: Five. Text-to-speech reads exactly what you type.
So you write it the way you want it said.
Write 'roe-ass.' Not 'ROAS.'

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a specific example (writing 'roe-ass' instead of 'ROAS') which isn't a direct continuation of the general advice given in Beat N. A bridge is needed to make the transition smoother and more logical.

chunks.prompt: MEME SPIKE — Wall-E on a trash pile at sunset (Pixar still, reference use — copyrighted character used as meme per ASSET_RULES.md carve-out). The absurdity of a robot taking instructions literally lands on top of the beat. Full-frame.

beats.narration: Otherwise it says each letter out loud. Like a roll call.

chunks.prompt: Horizontal split. Left side: human head icon with thought bubble labeled JUDGMENT. Right side: gear-icon pipeline labeled DETERMINISTIC. Arrow flows left to right.

beats.narration: Six. One stage in the pipeline is deliberately not automatable.
Turning messy raw material into a script needs judgment.
What's the practical question. What's the turning point. What's the one thing the viewer should come away with.
Mechanize that and you get generic.
Leave it human. The rest of the pipeline takes over from there.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 provides the answer to the implicit question raised by Beat N, but a bridge is needed to explicitly connect the two. · Beat N+1 introduces a list of practical questions without a clear setup, requiring a bridge to connect the idea of judgment to the specific questions. · Beat N+1 introduces a new concept ('mechanize that and you get generic') that doesn't naturally follow from the core message or key takeaway discussed in beat N, requiring a bridge to connect the ideas. · Beat N+1 introduces a new idea (leaving it human) without a clear connection to the previous thought (mechanizing leads to generic outcomes). · Beat N+1 introduces a new topic (economics) that is not a direct continuation of the pipeline discussion, requiring a bridge to pivot the focus.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Itemized receipt visual: Images $2, Voice $0, Animation $0, Render $0. Hand-drawn price tags.

beats.narration: Before the layers. The economics.
Per five-minute video.
Images — about fifty illustrations on kie.ai — roughly one to three dollars.
Voice — Google Cloud's Chirp3-HD text-to-speech. Free within the monthly tier.
Animation and render — both run locally. Zero cloud cost.

narration.notes: Beat N sets up a discussion about the economics, but Beat N+1 jumps to a specific metric without providing the necessary context or transition. · Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information about the cost of images, which is not a direct continuation of the thought about the length of the video. · The transition from images to voice is a new topic that the viewer is unlikely to have anticipated after learning about image costs. · Beat N+1 introduces a new topic (local animation and rendering) that is not directly related to the text-to-speech service mentioned in beat N, requiring a bridge to connect the two concepts.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Single-beat deadpan. A coffee cup next to a small receipt. The receipt shows one underlined total at the bottom reading exactly: 'TOTAL: $2'. No other line items, no other numbers visible anywhere in the image. Minimal, hand-drawn feel.

beats.narration: Total per video: roughly the price of a coffee.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a comparison with a much higher cost, which requires a bridge to make the transition smooth and clear.

chunks.prompt: Three bar-chart bars: HUMAN ANIMATOR (tall), DIY ADOBE (medium), SPOOLCAST (tiny). Height dramatic.

beats.narration: Hiring a human animator for a five-minute explainer runs five hundred to two thousand dollars.
Adobe plus Descript plus stock footage — fifty a month in subscriptions and a dozen hours of manual work.
Spoolcast is an order of magnitude cheaper than either path.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new topic (the layers of Spoolcast) without a clear connection to the cost-effectiveness mentioned in beat N.

chunks.summary: four layers intro — name + job + relationship
chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: A clean hand-drawn pipeline diagram. Four labeled boxes stacked horizontally, left to right: IMAGE (with a small paintbrush icon), ANIMATION (with a small motion-arrow icon), VOICE (with a small speech-bubble icon), RENDER (with a small mp4 play-button icon). Thin arrows connecting each box to the next. At the far right, a single larger mp4 thumbnail icon with a play button. Minimal stick-figure builder off to one side just observing the flow.

beats.narration: Four layers.
Image makes the pictures. Animation gives them motion. Voice narrates. Render stitches it all into an mp4.
Each does one thing. Together they make the video.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new topic without clearly connecting to the list of layers mentioned in beat N, requiring a bridge to guide the viewer.

chunks.prompt: JSON request visual flying into a kie.ai cloud icon, PNG returning. Clean.

beats.narration: Image layer.
One AI illustration per chunk.
The service is kie.ai. Their nano-banana-2 model.
Each image is one HTTP request — a JSON body with a prompt and an image_input array.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept without a clear transition from 'image layer,' which the viewer may not immediately connect. · Beat N+1 introduces new information about a specific service and model without a clear connection to the previous statement about illustrations per chunk. · Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information about how the model works, which is not a direct continuation of simply identifying the service and the model. · The viewer is left wondering about the purpose or benefit of this setup, which is not directly addressed by beat N+1.

chunks.prompt: Three mismatched-style tiles side by side. Same subject, totally different rendering.

beats.narration: The problem this solves.
If you ask a model for fifty illustrations, you get fifty art styles.
Scene one is a cartoon. Scene two is a watercolor. Scene three is photorealistic.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a specific example of the problem (varying art styles) without clearly connecting it to the general problem mentioned in Beat N. · Beat N+1 introduces a solution to a problem that was not explicitly stated in Beat N, requiring a bridge to connect the visual differences to the solution.

chunks.prompt: 3x3 grid composite of real pilot C1-C9 nano-1k PNGs. Proof the technique produces visually consistent scenes.

beats.narration: The fix is called image-ref chaining.
First generation establishes a style anchor.
Every subsequent request passes that anchor back in.
The model matches the existing style instead of inventing a new one.
Fifty scenes. One artist.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept that the viewer is likely curious about after learning about image-ref chaining, requiring a bridge to connect the two. · Beat N+1 introduces a new idea about style matching, which is not a direct consequence or continuation of the concept of passing the anchor back in every request. · Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information about the scale of the project (fifty scenes) and the efficiency of the process (one artist) without a clear connection to the consistency of style mentioned in beat N. · The transition from the accomplishment of fifty scenes by one artist to the concept of an animation layer is not immediately clear and requires a bridge to connect the two ideas.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Still image next to a yawning stick figure viewer. Clear 'boring' signal.

beats.narration: Animation layer.
A still image held on screen for eleven seconds while someone talks is dead air.
The image needs to appear over time.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (dead air from a still image) without a clear connection to the concept of an animation layer introduced in Beat N. · Beat N introduces a problem (making the image appear over time), and Beat N+1 provides a naive fix, but the transition could be smoother with a bridge that sets up the attempt.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: A mechanical conveyor-belt mechanism with a big solid rectangular wipe bar sliding across the frame in a straight horizontal line. Sharp edge. Obvious machinery: gears, arrows showing the direction, a clunky computer-generated feel. A small stick figure on the side looks unimpressed, arms crossed. Label: 'CSS WIPE'.

beats.narration: The naive fix is a CSS wipe.
Robotic. Up-down or left-right. Obvious every time.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 elaborates on the problem with the naive fix, but it could be clearer by directly addressing why the CSS wipe is problematic. · The transition from the robotic nature of the CSS wipe to a Python script solution is a clear problem-solution bridge, but the existing narration does not explicitly connect the two, making a bridge necessary.

chunks.prompt: Heatmap visualization: the same image colored by reveal time. Big regions blue (early), small details red (late). With a label 'reveal-time map.'

beats.narration: The real fix is a Python script that computes a per-pixel reveal-time map.
For every pixel, pick a time between zero and the chunk's duration when it should become visible.
Connected shapes reveal in parallel.
Big shapes start early. Small details fill in later. Everything finishes together.

narration.notes: The viewer is likely wondering about the behavior of connected pixels after hearing about individual pixel timings, and the next beat introduces a new concept that needs a bridge to connect the dots. · Beat N+1 introduces new information about the timing and structure of the shapes, which is not a direct continuation of the idea that connected shapes reveal in parallel. · Beat N+1 introduces a comparison to an artist speed-painting, which is orthogonal to the viewer's likely thought about the timing of the artistic process described in Beat N.

chunks.prompt: A human hand holding a paintbrush, painting rapidly across a canvas with visible brush strokes going in multiple directions. The canvas shows a partially-completed illustration emerging with multiple strokes appearing at once, not a single straight line. Motion lines around the hand showing speed. Warm, organic, hand-made feel. Label: 'ARTIST, NOT MACHINE'.

beats.narration: Looks like an artist speed-painting. Not a computer wiping.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a technical detail (OpenCV) that is orthogonal to the visual observation in Beat N, requiring a bridge to connect the two.

chunks.prompt: Python logo + OpenCV logo, input PNG to folder of numbered output frames. Padlock icon for determinism.

beats.narration: The script uses OpenCV, a computer vision library.
Runs locally. No AI tokens. No network calls. No randomness.
Same image in, same frames out. Always.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces new information about the script's operational environment, which is not a direct consequence or continuation of the fact that it uses OpenCV. · The transition from a consistent visual output to a voice layer is an orthogonal jump, as it introduces a new element without explaining how it relates to the previous statement.

chunks.prompt: Microphone icon labeled 'Puck' with a 1.2x speed indicator.

beats.narration: Voice layer.
Google Cloud Chirp3-HD text-to-speech.
The voice called Puck. Played at 1.2x speed.

narration.notes: Beat N introduces the concept of a 'voice layer' without providing context, and beat N+1 introduces a specific technology, which doesn't directly answer the viewer's likely question about what the voice layer is. · Beat N introduces the product, and Beat N+1 jumps to a specific feature without context, which can be disorienting for the viewer. · Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information about the default speed being robotic, which is not a direct consequence or continuation of the 1.2x speed statement.

chunks.prompt: Two waveform rows with label: top 'Puck 1.0x — robotic', bottom 'Puck 1.2x — podcast host'. Real audio plays back both for A/B compare.

beats.narration: Default speed sounds like a robot.
1.2x sounds like a real podcast host.
That's the whole trick.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a comparison with an earlier pick, which is not a direct continuation of the trick explained in Beat N, requiring a bridge to connect the two.

chunks.prompt: Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. No shading, no gradients, no texture. Bold black ink outlines on every shape. Cyanide-and-Happiness / XKCD visual family. Charmingly amateurish notebook-doodle vibe. No text inside the drawing unless the scene explicitly calls for a short UI label. Scene: Two labeled microphones side-by-side. ElevenLabs with $$$, Chirp3-HD with $0. Checkmark on Chirp3-HD.

beats.narration: The earlier pick was ElevenLabs. Marginally better. Hundreds of dollars a month at daily-video pace.
Chirp3-HD is free at this volume. Close enough nobody notices.

narration.notes: The transition to 'Render layer' introduces a new concept that is not a direct consequence, question, or solution to the previous beat, thus requiring a bridge.

chunks.prompt: Terminal with 'npx remotion render' command being typed, mp4 appearing in folder below. Remotion + React logos.

beats.narration: Render layer.
Remotion. A React-based video framework.
Each scene is a React component that renders a frame.
Running one command — npx remotion render — produces an mp4.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (Remotion) without directly addressing or expanding on the concept of a render layer, creating a disconnect for the viewer. · Beat N+1 introduces a new step in the process that the viewer might not immediately connect to the information in beat N without a bridging statement.

chunks.prompt: Terminal-styled image showing the `npx remotion render` command with real-looking output: bundling, metadata, progress bar at 92%, success messages. Dark theme. Monospace font. Designed to pan across slightly for motion in Remotion rather than a literal screen recording.

beats.narration: Headless. No browser opens. It just runs.
Deterministic. Same inputs, same mp4. Always.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new characteristic (deterministic behavior) that, while related to the technology, does not directly follow from the headless nature. The bridge helps to link the two features more naturally. · The viewer is likely thinking about the reliability and consistency of the system after beat N, but beat N+1 introduces a new concept (the layers) without a clear connection, making it an orthogonal jump.

chunks.prompt: Full pipeline diagram: SCRIPT at top, four layer boxes below, each with a swap/replace icon.

beats.narration: Four layers. Each doing one narrow thing.
Nothing improvises.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new idea (nothing improvises) that is not a direct consequence or continuation of the idea that each layer has a specific role. · Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (the pipeline's output) without directly answering the implicit question raised by Beat N, which requires a bridge to connect the two thoughts.

chunks.prompt: Simple arrow pointing right, setting up the reveal.

beats.narration: Here's what the whole pipeline produced.

narration.notes: The viewer is expecting a more detailed explanation or a direct consequence of the pipeline's output, but the next beat introduces a specific metric without a clear connection, making it feel like an orthogonal jump.

chunks.prompt: 20-25 sec of pilot-full-v2.mp4 with its audio. Section showing reveal + style-locked scenes + recognizable narration.

Loose hand-drawn black ink line art with basic flat fill colors on pure white background (#FFFFFF). Simple stick-figure characters — round bald heads, dot eyes, expressive simple mouths, thin curved limbs. Flat basic colors only. Core palette: orange for clothing, peach for skin tone, gray for devices, white background. Additional colors (green, blue, red) only when the specific scene description mentions them. You did the building. The content got made.

beats.narration: Spoolcast. The rendering engine for passive content.

transcript: You build things. It came out great. Now what? Getting attention for what you built is a separate job. Different skills. Different time. Different energy. Even if you have all three — skills, time, energy — the harder problem is: most of what gets built doesn't reach anyone. The obvious fix is to have an AI make the video for you. That produces slop. Style drifts from scene to scene. Pacing is random. You can't iterate without the whole thing regenerating into a different video. This is spoolcast. It's an architecture for making real short-form illustrated video. Built for people who build things and don't want marketing to be a second job. In the next seven minutes, we'll walk through the pipeline that made that clip for under three dollars. And the agent layer that runs it in the background while you keep building. The fix isn't a smarter AI. It's a division of labor. The generic approach asks one model to do everything. Story. Script. Images. Animation. Camera. Timing. Rendering. When anything's wrong, there's no atomic unit you can change and re-run. That's why the output feels slopped together. Structurally, it was. In spoolcast, there's exactly one atomic driver. The script. Specifically, a shot list — a spreadsheet with one row per sentence the narrator will say, grouped into scenes. Change one row. Only the artifacts downstream of that row get rebuilt. Everything else stays untouched. Every other layer has exactly one job. And one rule. It cannot improvise. Here's what specifically keeps the script from being slop. One. Images are grouped by chunk, not by beat. A chunk is one to six sentences that share a visual moment. One illustration covers the whole chunk. Saves the budget. Keeps the eye from whipping around. Two. The image doesn't illustrate a sentence. It shows the chunk's narrative throughline. A chunk about ad saturation gets one image of an ad-wall. Not a picture of every individual word. Three. Chunks have a hard cap around fifteen seconds. Past that, the static image goes dead. Split it. New image. Four. Every sentence has to survive on its own. If a line only makes sense when you read the one before it, that's an essay. Rewrite until each beat can be lifted out and still land. Five. Text-to-speech reads exactly what you type. So you write it the way you want it said. Write 'roe-ass.' Not 'ROAS.' Otherwise it says each letter out loud. Like a roll call. Six. One stage in the pipeline is deliberately not automatable. Turning messy raw material into a script needs judgment. What's the practical question. What's the turning point. What's the one thing the viewer should come away with. Mechanize that and you get generic. Leave it human. The rest of the pipeline takes over from there. Before the layers. The economics. Per five-minute video. Images — about fifty illustrations on kie.ai — roughly one to three dollars. Voice — Google Cloud's Chirp3-HD text-to-speech. Free within the monthly tier. Animation and render — both run locally. Zero cloud cost. Total per video: roughly the price of a coffee. Hiring a human animator for a five-minute explainer runs five hundred to two thousand dollars. Adobe plus Descript plus stock footage — fifty a month in subscriptions and a dozen hours of manual work. Spoolcast is an order of magnitude cheaper than either path. Four layers. Image makes the pictures. Animation gives them motion. Voice narrates. Render stitches it all into an mp4. Each does one thing. Together they make the video. Image layer. One AI illustration per chunk. The service is kie.ai. Their nano-banana-2 model. Each image is one HTTP request — a JSON body with a prompt and an image_input array. The problem this solves. If you ask a model for fifty illustrations, you get fifty art styles. Scene one is a cartoon. Scene two is a watercolor. Scene three is photorealistic. The fix is called image-ref chaining. First generation establishes a style anchor. Every subsequent request passes that anchor back in. The model matches the existing style instead of inventing a new one. Fifty scenes. One artist. Animation layer. A still image held on screen for eleven seconds while someone talks is dead air. The image needs to appear over time. The naive fix is a CSS wipe. Robotic. Up-down or left-right. Obvious every time. The real fix is a Python script that computes a per-pixel reveal-time map. For every pixel, pick a time between zero and the chunk's duration when it should become visible. Connected shapes reveal in parallel. Big shapes start early. Small details fill in later. Everything finishes together. Looks like an artist speed-painting. Not a computer wiping. The script uses OpenCV, a computer vision library. Runs locally. No AI tokens. No network calls. No randomness. Same image in, same frames out. Always. Voice layer. Google Cloud Chirp3-HD text-to-speech. The voice called Puck. Played at 1.2x speed. Default speed sounds like a robot. 1.2x sounds like a real podcast host. That's the whole trick. The earlier pick was ElevenLabs. Marginally better. Hundreds of dollars a month at daily-video pace. Chirp3-HD is free at this volume. Close enough nobody notices. Render layer. Remotion. A React-based video framework. Each scene is a React component that renders a frame. Running one command — npx remotion render — produces an mp4. Headless. No browser opens. It just runs. Deterministic. Same inputs, same mp4. Always. Four layers. Each doing one narrow thing. Nothing improvises. Here's what the whole pipeline produced. Five minutes. Forty-four chunks. AI voice. AI images. AI-written title. AI-generated thumbnail. Zero overlay improvisation. Rendered headless. One command. One thing left — and this piece is still being built as of this video. Someone has to decide there's a video worth making and write the brief. The plan is to delegate that too. The design is an agent that lives in your chat room. In this project — Zara. An Animabot running on a Matrix server. Built and talking in chat already. Not yet wired up to spoolcast. Once it's wired up, Zara will watch every signal the builder generates while working. Commits pushed to GitHub. New tracker entries. Chat messages about a milestone. A thread where the builder explained what they just figured out. When the signals line up, Zara writes the brief. A few paragraphs. Project name. Practical question. Turning point. Core message. The brief goes to the editorial agent. The editorial agent returns a shot list. Spoolcast renders. Zara drops the finished mp4 back in the chat with a short note. From the builder's side, once this is live, the whole thing will feel like this. You ship a thing. You go back to building. A few hours later, a video about what you just built appears in your chat. You won't write the script. You won't generate images. You won't edit anything. You did the building. The content got made. The 'mostly' in 'mostly automated' is the editorial agent that still needs real context. Because the alternative is a generic summary nobody watches. Mostly automated. With one intentional judgment layer that keeps the output from being slop. Using AI video to get attention used to mean one of two things. Producing slop. Or spending a week on every video. It doesn't anymore. Scope the AI narrowly. Let deterministic code handle the rest. Put an agent in the chat room to pull the trigger. The builder keeps building. The content gets made. Passively. And mostly automated. Spoolcast. The rendering engine for passive content.

showcase.notes: V1 of the spoolcast pair — the technical explainer.
